ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general peanut food product class; no specific baked/unsalted peanut subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers dry-roasted peanuts without salt, which is the closest defensible match for oven-baked unsalted peanuts.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for baked unsalted peanuts; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of baked unsalted peanuts is approximately 30g (one small handful), providing around 176 kcal, 7.3g protein, 14.9g fat, and 6.4g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Baked peanut, unsalted — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), record F09030009
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F09030009 for baked peanut, unsalted. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 784424, Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, without salt) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: Peanuts, roasted, salted — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney (Atkinson et al., 2008; Foster-Powell et al., 2002)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 14 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for roasted peanuts (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008). Unsalted baked peanuts are nutritionally equivalent to dry-roasted peanuts for GI purposes; salt content does not affect GI. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (14 × 6.38g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.9 (rounded). Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate 21.26g minus dietary fibre 8.4g = 12.86g per 100g; per 30g serving = 3.86g. GL = (14 × 3.86) / 100 = 0.54, rounded to 0.9 using total carbohydrate basis per standard HPB convention: (14 × 6.38) / 100 = 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6mg/100g) with high potassium (~705mg/100g), yielding an excellent Na:K ratio consistent with DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Unsalted baked peanuts are appropriate for individuals with hypertension; limit to ~30g per serving to manage caloric and saturated fat intake, and strictly avoid salted or flavoured variants which can contain 400–600mg sodium per 100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 14) driven by high protein (24.35g), fat (49.66g), and dietary fibre (8.4g) that substantially attenuates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baked unsalted peanuts are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their very low GI of 14 and high fibre content; a 30g portion provides a glycemic load of less than 1, but caloric density (587 kcal/100g) warrants portion control and avoidance of honey-roasted or sugar-coated variants.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dominant unsaturated fat profile (MUFA 24.43g + PUFA 15.69g per 100g) with zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and high dietary fibre (8.4g) supporting LDL reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baked unsalted peanuts have a cardioprotective fat profile with high MUFA and PUFA and zero trans fat; saturated fat at 6.89g/100g is moderate, so a 30g serving (~2.1g saturated fat) is within cardiovascular dietary guidelines, and regular moderate consumption is associated with reduced CVD risk in epidemiological studies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High MUFA/PUFA ratio with moderate saturated fat (6.89g/100g); peanuts are a common allergen and may provoke immune responses in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Peanuts may be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given their dominant anti-inflammatory MUFA/PUFA profile and high fibre; however, walnuts are preferable due to higher omega-3 content, and individuals with peanut allergy must avoid entirely.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked peanut, unsalted (HPB Singapore record F09030009) refers to whole peanuts (Arachis hypogaea) that have been oven-baked or dry-roasted without the addition of salt or any seasoning. The result is a crunchy, nutty-flavoured snack with a slightly deeper roasted aroma compared to raw peanuts. Nutritionally, per 100g they provide 587 kcal, 24.35g protein, 49.66g total fat (predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats at ~40g combined), 21.26g carbohydrate, 8.4g dietary fibre, and only 6mg sodium. The very low glycemic index of 14 — driven by the combined effect of high protein, fat, and fibre — makes these peanuts highly suitable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The negligible sodium content and high potassium (~705mg/100g) provide an excellent sodium-to-potassium ratio aligned with DASH diet principles, supporting blood pressure management in hypertensive individuals. The dominant unsaturated fat profile (zero trans fat, zero cholesterol) is cardioprotective. Peanuts are a major allergen and must be avoided by individuals with peanut allergy. A typical serving is approximately 30g (one small handful). They are classified as minimally processed, plant-based, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Are baked peanuts good for diabetics?
Can I eat peanuts if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in baked peanuts?
What is the GI of peanuts?
How much protein in baked peanuts?
What is a healthy serving size for peanuts?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F09030009 (confidence 0.95 per input metadata). Saturated fat, fatty acid breakdown, potassium, and micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 784424 (dry-roasted peanuts without salt), which is an exact category match. GI of 14 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for roasted peanuts — a defensible analogous match for unsalted baked peanuts.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.89g), monounsaturated fat (24.43g), polyunsaturated fat (15.69g), trans fat (0.0g), potassium (705mg), cholesterol (0mg), and micronutrients (vitamins B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 784424 (Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, without salt) where HPB Foodo does not report these fields. 2. GI value of 14 is from the University of Sydney GI Database for roasted peanuts; salt content does not affect GI, making this a defensible analogous match for unsalted baked peanuts. 3. Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as (GI 14 × total carbohydrate per serving 6.38g) / 100 = 0.9. 4. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a generic international snack food. Mandatory multilingual local-name fields are therefore not applicable and are set to null. 5. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained; all -1 defaults from the template have been replaced with sourced numeric values. 6. Rheumatoid arthritis condition retained from input as an additional_conditions entry; the input record incorrectly set suitable=false while the reason text described a MUFA/PUFA carve-out supporting suitability — this has been resolved as moderate concern with nuanced guidance. 7. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301491 is the closest defensible match for a peanut food product; no exact baked/unsalted subclass exists in FoodOn as of 2026-04-16.

Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'exact' as A00LC is an exact match for dry-roasted unsalted peanuts, which is equivalent to baked unsalted peanuts.
